Project Kratos
Windows Anti-Ransomware MiniFilter Driver

SN
Simon Ngoy · Security Researcher · Red Team / Windows Internals

Introduction

In today's cyber threat landscape, ransomware remains one of the most critical challenges to enterprise resilience. Faced with automated attacks that encrypt data at high speed - whether through direct in-place encryption or via temporary files - traditional antivirus solutions based on static signatures are showing their limitations. Attackers easily circumvent these barriers through polymorphism or obfuscation.

The true battleground for this detection lies at the heart of the operating system: Kernel mode. Unlike agents running in user mode - which are subject to context transition latency and limited visibility - a Windows file system filter driver (Minifilter) offers atomic and synchronous interception. It allows for the analysis, suspension, or rejection of I/O requests on NTFS/ReFS volumes in real time, even before changes are committed to the disk.

Kratos Design Philosophy

Rather than attempting to restore encrypted files (which causes race conditions and massive I/O overhead), Kratos sacrifices backup in favor of absolute prevention through digital fingerprinting. By limiting initial infection to a maximum of 5 files, Kratos captures the binary's hash to permanently immunize the system - guaranteeing 0 affected files on any re-execution attempt.

The Test Laboratory: Hercules Ransomware

To validate Kratos against real-world scenarios, a controlled test environment was set up. The threat is simulated by a custom-designed ransomware called Hercules, designed to faithfully reproduce the two main data destruction strategies used by attackers.

2.1 Hercules' Encryption Strategies

Hercules alternates between two distinct operating modes to stress Kratos's context structures:

  • Hercules v1 - Substitution cipher , indirect lifecycle: reads the original file, writes encrypted data to a temporary .tmp file, deletes the original, then renames the temporary file. Generates a distinct I/O stream pattern.
  • Hercules v2 - In-Place Encryption , more aggressive: directly opens the target file and overwrites its original sectors using successive WriteFile calls - no intermediate files created. Maximum I/O pressure on the driver.

2.2 The Evolution Toward Extension Polymorphism

Initially, ransomware relied on predictable file extensions (.tmp, .locked). The latest generation bypasses this basic signature by generating completely random strings to disguise their files (e.g., document.docx.g7x9a2).

In response, Kratos no longer validates a blacklist of fixed extensions. Instead, its renaming callback analyzes the syntactic structure and frequency of extension changes. If a process sequentially applies a non-standard extension to multiple files, Kratos immediately raises a SUSPICIOUS_RENAME alert, triggering threat score evaluation regardless of the name chosen by the attacker.

Kratos Technical Architecture

Kratos.sys is developed using the Windows File System Minifilter architecture. This model, managed by FltMgr.sys, eliminates the need for older unstable techniques such as SSDT table hooking or chaining legacy filter drivers. It guarantees clean and standardized interception of I/O requests.

3.1 Recording and Lifecycle of I/O

Upon driver loading, DriverEntry initializes security structures and registers the mini-filter via FltRegisterFilter. Kratos then dynamically attaches to active storage volumes (NTFS/ReFS) at a specific altitude - the hierarchical layer defined by Microsoft for anti-ransomware tools.

Kratos declares a callback table to intercept the most critical I/O request packets:

IRP Type Callback Purpose
IRP_MJ_CREATE PreCreate Identify the calling process identity before a file handle is even opened
IRP_MJ_WRITE PreWrite Inspect data being written to measure entropy
IRP_MJ_SET_INFORMATION PreSetInformation Monitor renaming operations (extension changes) and file deletions

3.2 Context Management: Performance Without Recalculation

One of the major challenges in the Windows kernel is avoiding recalculating file or process information every time an I/O operation passes through. Kratos uses Filter Contexts - custom data structures allocated in non-paged memory and attached directly to OS-managed objects.

KS_PROCESS_CONTEXT - allocated upon detection or start-up of a process. Stores PID, cumulative threat score (ThreatScore), and the immunization flag (pre-calculated hash):

KS_STREAMHANDLE_CONTEXT and KS_FILE_CONTEXT - associated with each file opening instance (FileObject). Allows Kratos to remember the state of a file between the time it is opened, modified, then renamed or closed:

3.3 IRQL Management and Synchronization Primitives

Windows kernel-mode development imposes a strict constraint absent from user-mode: Interrupt Request Levels (IRQLs). A mini-filter primarily operates at two levels: PASSIVE_LEVEL (thread can be interrupted) and APC_LEVEL (asynchronous procedure calls). I/O completion routines can be invoked at DISPATCH_LEVEL or higher - strictly prohibiting access to paged memory.

Primitive IRQL Constraint Use Case in Kratos
FAST_MUTEX ≤ APC_LEVEL only Protects data structures during resource-intensive operations (log writes, config access). Faster acquisition than standard mutexes.
KSPIN_LOCK Raises to DISPATCH_LEVEL Rapidly updates ThreatScore during massive write streams. Limited to ultra-short operations to avoid OS freeze.
ERESOURCE PASSIVE_LEVEL (shared read) Reader/Writer lock for the banned binaries hash table. Dozens of threads can simultaneously check if a PID is banned.

The Evolution of Detection Mechanisms

The development of Kratos was marked by complex technical challenges that profoundly redefined its architecture - from a classic reactive approach with a backup mechanism to the current model focused on pure, high-performance prevention.

4.1 Phase 1: Entropy Measurement and the Double-Nucleus Trap

The first version of Kratos relied on Shannon entropy calculations to assess whether buffers passing through PreWrite contained encrypted data (characterized by entropy close to the absolute maximum: 8.0).

Two major pitfalls quickly crippled this approach:

  • Floating-point instability: The FPU/XMM registers are not saved by default during context switches in the kernel. Using double slowed the driver and skewed display metrics with risk of crash (Bug Check).
  • Integer division scaling trap: The initial evaluation suffered from a scaling conflict between integer sampling buffer values and floating thresholds. The entropy delta remained mathematically stuck at inconsistent values.
Solution: Fixed-Point Arithmetic

Kratos migrated to a fixed-point model using exclusively ULONG integers. An entropy of 7.35 is now treated as the integer 735. High-frequency I/O calculations execute directly in CPU registers in nanoseconds, without any risk of instability. Entropy is measured via a 512-entry lookup table (KratosEntropyLUT512).

4.2 Feedback: Why the Backup System Was Abandoned (KS_BackupToADS)

Initially, Kratos incorporated a resilience mechanism: upon detecting a suspicious write, the driver intercepted the I/O and made a backup copy of the healthy file in an Alternate Data Stream (ADS) attached to the original file. Confrontation with Hercules v1 revealed fundamental flaws:

  • Race Conditions: Hercules v1 operated at lightning speed. Kratos attempted to read file objects which, in the next microsecond, were already renamed or destroyed by the malware - causing waves of I/O errors ( 0xC0000034 / STATUS_OBJECT_NAME_NOT_FOUND).
  • Performance collapse: Doubling each write operation with a read/copy operation on disk created a massive bottleneck, slowing down the entire OS.

4.3 The Architectural Pivot: Absolute Prevention

A significant technical choice was made: sacrifice restoration in favor of surgical blocking speed. Kratos now allows ransomware to manipulate a strict quota of maximum 5 files. During this short window, the behavioral engine validates the threat by accumulating indicators. As soon as ThreatScore >= 80, Kratos freezes execution and switches to its marking and final immunization phase.

Preventive Phase: Blocking by Digital Fingerprint

Once the architectural pivot is made, Kratos moves from behavioral monitoring to a strict immunization policy based on binary identity - avoiding the trap of simple blocking by access path (trivially circumvented by renaming).

5.1 Partial Hash Sampling to Preserve Performance

Calculating the full cryptographic hash (SHA-256) of a large binary every time a process is detected would saturate I/O bandwidth. Kratos implements Partial Hashing (Sampling): only the first 64 KB of the file are analyzed via the ZwReadFile API. This area contains the DOS header, PE header, section table, entry point, and rich header - the most statistically discriminating bytes. The calculation takes only a few microseconds.

5.2 Flash Indexing via FNV-1a

To instantly check whether the calling process is banned at each PreCreate call, Kratos uses the non-cryptographic FNV-1a (Fowler-Noll-Vo) algorithm for its internal in-memory indexing table:

hash = (hash ⊕ byte) × FNV_prime
Thanks to the mathematical simplicity of FNV-1a, searching the table for banned processes takes constant O(1) time.

5.3 The Masquerading Trap and Path Validation

To avoid detection, ransomware can rename itself to svchost.exe and run from the desktop. If the driver relied solely on the image name, it would mistakenly trust the process. Kratos counters this with absolute path verification in the kernel namespace (e.g., \Device\HarddiskVolume3\Windows\System32\svchost.exe). If the path reveals a process running outside the official system directory, Kratos detects the impersonation and subjects it to FNV-1a hashing upon the first entropy anomaly.

Results and Performance Evaluation

The effectiveness and performance impact of Kratos were rigorously evaluated against various Hercules variants. A custom binary was used to overcome the limitations of real ransomware samples (anti-debugging, anti-VM mechanisms), ensuring perfect repeatability.

6.1 Initial Attack Scenario (First Execution)

When Hercules is first launched on a test directory, the threat score crosses the critical threshold of 90 after only 3 to 5 corrupted files. Kratos freezes I/O, extracts the 64 KB partial hash, writes it to the FNV-1a table, and then issues the kill order for the malicious process. Over 99.5% of the filesystem is preserved in the first wave of the attack.

6.2 Resolution Scenario (Absolute Immunization)

During a re-execution attempt (the malware renamed to explorer.exe to deceive the driver), Kratos intercepts the call at PreCreate. The path verification raises a spoofing alert, the FNV-1a table is queried in O(1) time, and Kratos immediately rejects the I/O:

C - Kratos immunization response
 FNV-1a table hit: binary already blacklisted
Data->IoStatus.Status = STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED;
return FLT_PREOP_COMPLETE;

Result: 0 encrypted files on re-execution attempt
Perfect Immunization Score

The ransomware is completely paralyzed. The result of this second attempt shows a perfect score of 0 encrypted files. The binary identity is permanently stored in the FNV-1a table, immune to renaming or path changes.

6.3 Systemic Transparency: ~0% Overhead

Thanks to surgical optimizations, Kratos achieves total transparency - CPU and memory performance of the OS remain strictly identical whether the driver is active or unloaded. Comparative tests using I/O stress tools (Diskspd, Iometer) show throughput and CPU curves that perfectly overlap with and without Kratos.

  • Opportunistic sampling in PreWriteOnly the first waves of writing are analyzed before validation of the context. Once a process is whitelisted, it bypasses all entropy checks.
  • Pre-allocated structures in non-paged memoryElimination of dynamic memory allocations in critical paths. Context structures are initialized at driver load time.
  • Integer arithmetic hyper-optimizationFixed-point ULONG arithmetic executed directly in general registers - no FPU/XMM unit usage, no context-switch overhead.

Conclusion and Future Perspectives

Project Kratos demonstrates that modern and effective ransomware protection relies on the precision of its low-layer behavioral telemetry and the speed of its preventative blocking. With fewer than 5 files corrupted during the initial attack, a perfect score of 0 files affected on re-run, and ~0% OS overhead (comparable to industry-leading solutions like Sophos XDR/CryptoGuard), Kratos fulfills all design objectives.

Key Trade-Off

The deliberate sacrifice of the backup/restore mechanism in favor of absolute prevention speed is the architectural cornerstone of Kratos. Rather than trying to recover from an ongoing infection, Kratos never allows the infection to spread past the detection window.

7.1 Future Prospects

  • Rust Hybrid Architecture: Keep the core I/O filtering in kernel C (due to current Rust ecosystem immaturity for fine-grained FltMgr.sys management), while developing the user-mode management service in Rust for memory safety guarantees.
  • Image Notification Hardening: Couple the PreCreate logic with PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine to prevent memory mapping of the banned binary as soon as loading is attempted by the OS.

References

  • [1] P. Yosifovich, Windows Kernel Programming, 2nd ed. Patterns & Practices, 2023.
  • [2] Microsoft Corporation, "Windows File System Minifilter Drivers Architecture," Microsoft Learn, 2025.
  • [3] C. Shannon, "A Mathematical Theory of Communication," Bell System Technical Journal, 1948.
  • [4] M. Russinovich, D. A. Solomon, A. Ionescu, Windows Internals, Part 1 & 2 (7th Edition), Microsoft Press, 2017.
  • [5] Fowler-Noll-Vo Hash Function (FNV-1a), IETF Internet-Draft, 2024.
  • [6] Sophos Research, "CryptoGuard: Behavioral Anti-Ransomware Architecture," Sophos Whitepaper, 2024.
